Title: Hegseth fires back at Warren’s insider trading comments tied to Iran war: ‘No one owns me’
URL: https://www.foxnews.com/politics/hegseth-fires-back-warrens-insider-trading-comments-tied-iran-war-no-one-owns-me
Time Published: 2026-04-30T19:54:57Z
Full Content:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren, D-Mass., raises insider trading concerns tied to the Iran conflict, and War Secretary Pete Hegseth denied wrongdoing in a heated exchange. War Secretary Pete Hegseth forcefully denied allegations that he or others may have profited from the war with Iran during a heated Senate exchange Thursday, telling Sen. Elizabeth Warren, D-Mass., that "no one owns me" as she pressed him on what she said was suspicious trading activity tied to the conflict. Warren pointed to what she described as a pattern of large, well-timed oil trades occurring minutes before public announcements by President Donald Trump related to the war, suggesting the possibility that insiders were using nonpublic information to profit. Hegseth rejected the premise of the claims, insisting the department operated "completely above board. What happens in markets is not, in betting markets, is not something we're involved in," he said. The exchange reflects growing scrutiny in Washington over whether sensitive information tied to the Iran conflict could be used for financial gain and broader concerns about ethics rules governing senior officials and lawmakers. HEGSETH, CAINE FACE SENATE GRILLING ON IRAN WAR AS HORMUZ BLOCKADE RATTLES OIL MARKETS War Secretary Pete Hegseth forcefully denied allegations that he or others may have profited from the war with Iran during a heated Senate exchange Thursday. (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images) "In just the space of minutes, it looks like insiders have been making out like bandits, using secret information about the war," Warren said. She pressed Hegseth repeatedly on whether he had any explanation for the activity "other than insider trading." Hegseth declined to engage directly with the premise, saying the department had operated "completely above board" and emphasizing that his focus remained on military operations rather than financial markets. He added that safeguarding sensitive information is taken "very seriously." SECRETARY HEGSETH TUSSLES WITH DEMOCRATS OVER STRATEGY IN IRAN WAR "What happens in markets … is not something we're involved in," Hegseth said. The exchange escalated when Warren referenced reporting about Hegseth’s own financial dealings, including a claim that a broker attempted to invest in a defense-related fund ahead of the conflict. "That entire story is false," Hegseth responded. "Any insinuation that I have ever profited … I don't do it for money. I don't do it for profit. I don't do it for stocks. "No one owns me. No one owns this department. No one owns this president." Warren’s line of questioning follows recent reporting that raised questions about Hegseth’s financial dealings ahead of the Iran conflict. The Financial Times reported that a broker working on Hegseth’s behalf contacted BlackRock in February about making a multimillion-dollar investment in a fund tied to major defense contractors, including Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman, in the weeks before military action began. According to the report, the investment ultimately did not take place because the fund was not available to the broker at the time. The Pentagon has strongly denied the reporting, calling it "entirely false and fabricated," and Hegseth reiterated that denial during Thursday’s hearing. Hegseth and Warren (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images) "In just the space of minutes, it looks like insiders have been making out like bandits, using secret information about the war," Warren claimed. (Heather Diehl/Getty Images) Federal law places strict limits on defense officials’ investments to prevent conflicts of interest. Presidential appointees at the Pentagon generally are barred from owning or buying stock in top defense contractors that receive the largest government contracts, with narrow exceptions such as diversified funds. Ethics rules also require senior officials to avoid even the appearance of self-dealing and to disclose or seek approval for certain financial activity. Warren’s comments come amid a recent high-profile case involving a U.S. service member accused of profiting from nonpublic military information. Federal prosecutors charged Army Special Forces Master Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke with using classified intelligence about a covert operation to capture Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro to place bets on a prediction market, allegedly earning more than $400,000. Authorities allege Van Dyke was involved in planning the operation and used that knowledge to wager on its outcome before it became public, prompting charges including wire fraud and unlawful use of government information. Critics have pointed to a perceived double standard, noting that while a U.S. service member faces potential prison time for insider trading, enforcement of similar rules governing members of Congress historically has been less aggressive. Federal prosecutors charged Army Special Forces Master Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke with using classified intelligence about a covert operation to capture Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro to place bets on a prediction market, allegedly earning more than $400,000. (Gannon Ken Van Dyke/AP) "This man is facing decades in prison," Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla., told Fox News' "Ingraham Angle" Saturday. "Meanwhile, every single day on Capitol Hill, there are many members of Congress on both sides that are currently engaging in insider trading." CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Trump weighed in Saturday, comparing the case to baseball legend Pete Rose. "That's a little like Pete Rose," Trump said when asked about the case. "Pete Rose, they kept him out of the Hall of Fame for betting on his own team. Now, if he bet against his team, that would be no good, but he bet on his own team. I'll look into it." Title: Ethereum Analysis May 2026: Vitalik Buterin, ETH Accumulation & Bullish Price Forecast
URL: https://bitcoinfoundation.org/news/ethereum/ethereum-analysis-may-2026-vitalik-buterin-eth-accumulation-bullish-price-forecast/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T19:43:01Z
Full Content:
When May 2026 arrives, changes across Ethereum go deep into its bones. Instead of just small investors pushing talk, three forces now steer things: how founder Vitalik Buterin shifts his holdings, big players such as Tom Lee’s Bitmine buying up supply fast, along with unseen upgrades to the code itself. Since “Glamsterdam” went live – and “Hegotá” is waiting in the wings – pressure builds quietly beneath the surface. What comes next might shake ETH’s lowest value point hard enough to set terms for years after. What grabs blockchain detectives every time? Vitalik Buterin keeps moving. Fresh numbers from Arkham show something odd: his wallet, tagged “Vitalik.eth,” now floods with tokens tied to Internet noise. Project builders toss huge chunks of meme-driven coins straight into that account – no questions asked. Their logic? Seeing his name listed might just spark trust, like an invisible nod nobody gave but everyone sees. Movement there isn’t quiet – it echoes. Still, Buterin keeps doing the same thing every time. Instead of holding on, he moves step by step to cash out. Data from Lookonchain caught him turning vast amounts of random tokens – like ASTEROID OG and similar ones – into ETH. One day alone showed about $350,000 in untouched coins traded straight for Ethereum. vitalik.eth (@VitalikButerin) has been continuously selling the meme tokens he received for free, and it looks like he's clearing out his wallet.In the past 24 hours, he has gained 114,566 $USDC and 155 $ETH ($355K) by selling those free meme tokens.https://t.co/EWizSRolCi pic.twitter.com/9M2b80BUI8 Sales like these do more than one thing. One reason behind them is supporting causes Buterin cares about – think of projects tied to biotechnology or open science efforts built on blockchains. Instead of holding tokens, he trades meme-based ones for Ether, feeding what some might dismiss as playful money into something with a deeper function. In doing so, energy that could vanish into hype gets pulled toward stability. Each transaction quietly supports demand for ETH, even if only by a little. It also shows Ethereum stands for creation, not just quick wins or luck-driven plays. Read More: Top 5 Wild Ethereum Price Predictions for 2026: From Realistic to Moonshot Scenarios Even as Buterin takes center stage socially, real movement behind prices comes from quiet big players – those nameless giants and firms working unseen. A recent Lookonchain highlight stands out: around $850 million worth of ETH vanished from central exchanges in late April 2026, swept away by only a few major hands. Price may sit still or dip a little, yet strong buying underneath tells another tale – one where serious holders steadily pull supply offline. That tension between flat charts and hidden accumulation? It’s a textbook bullish split forming quietly beneath the surface. Out of nowhere, old crypto wallets from the 2014–2015 fundraising days of Ethereum have woken up. Arkham Intelligence spotted movement – dormant since back then, now active again. A single wallet pushed 10,000 ETH into staking after sitting still for 11 years. These long-gone holders are returning. Their timing hints at trust in what comes next. Ethereum’s planned improvements in 2026 seem like a turning point. For them, it might be the moment when earning rewards finally feels secure. It’s Tom Lee who stands out among big names in 2026, running things at Bitmine. Though many recognize him from CNBC spots and talk about market supercycles, what his company does on blockchain reveals deeper clues. His moves online paint a clearer picture than headlines ever could. Related: Ethereum Updates 2026: Upcoming Changes That Could Transform the Network Now shaping the backbone of digital systems, Bitmine started as just a trading hub. Holding close to 4.17 million ETH – that’s about 3.45 percent of every unit in existence – it sits on one of the densest personal stockpiles around. With more than 1.25 million ETH locked into staking, its role shifts beyond storage. Functioning much like a reserve authority within open networks, it fuels access to funds while pulling in close to $100 million each year through staked returns. WHALES ARE DOUBLING DOWN ON ETHThese whale addresses just purchased a further $44.8M of ETH this morning.Judging by the purchase pattern, this appears to be Bitmine. Is Tom Lee doubling down? pic.twitter.com/VXWbvC2Oj9 Come 2026, Lee’s argument rests on one core idea: too little Ethereum floating around when so much gets locked away. Big players such as Bitmine and BlackRock stash ETH in staking pools or exchange-traded funds (ETFs) – taking it off the open market. Meanwhile, busy times on the network destroy more of it via EIP-1559, slowly shrinking what remains tradeable. With fewer coins showing up on exchanges, pressure builds beneath the price. That tightening pool, not speculation, may drive Ethereum into the $5,000–$7,000 zone by year-end 2026. Scarcity whispers louder than hype. One step at a time, Ethereum shifts beyond what once felt like growing pains. In 2026, plans reach further than any before. Maturity takes shape through speed, stability, and strength. What began as an experiment now stands more solid – less trial, more function. Early in April 2026 came Glamsterdam, bringing locked-in Proposer-Builder Separation – known as ePBS. Up until then, building blocks leaned toward central control, easily twisted by strong MEV-focused bots. Thanks to ePBS, that task now spreads across many players, blocking one actor from jumping ahead of trades. Confidence among big financial users in Ethereum’s fairness got stronger because of it. Later in 2026, the path toward true independence turns on the Hegotá update. Instead of today’s Merkle Patricia Trees, it brings in Verkle Trees – shrinking what nodes must check. The shift sounds subtle, yet cuts proof sizes sharply. Though buried deep in code, its impact ripples outward fast. Running an Ethereum node might soon fit inside a regular phone. That happens because stateless clients reduce the amount of data that needs to be saved. A lightweight setup could work without requiring large amounts of memory. Some devices never handled nodes before – they can now join. Less storage demand opens new possibilities. Old limits start fading when tech adapts like this. One step beyond, Hegotá teams up with the ZKVM effort to stretch system capacity past ten million actions each second across both base layer and expansion layers. What unfolds next depends on how tightly these parts link together under pressure. Close to May 2026, forces start lining up – quiet at first, then harder to ignore. One thing follows another, building pressure beneath the surface. Prices begin to twitch, not by accident. Each piece adds weight, even if unseen. What’s been slow to show now pushes into view. Word has it that after seeing what Bitmine did, a few medium-sized pension plans might soon shift 1 to 2 percent of their holdings into ETH ETFs. Not everyone is moving at once, yet the pattern suggests quiet momentum behind the scenes. Every time he trades memecoins into ETH, Vitalik nudges perception – quietly shaping how people see the token. Not as noise, but something that builds. His moves don’t shout; they settle into view like weathered facts. Slowly, the idea sticks: value flows here because it works. What was playful becomes weighty without announcement. Action after action piles up, meaning. The pattern isn’t loud – it’s just there, like footprints leading somewhere obvious only later. Surprisingly low exchange liquidity shows up in Lookonchain figures – Ethereum’s presence on trading platforms hasn’t been this small since 2016. While most assets sit elsewhere, the trend quietly highlights a shift over the years. Notably, the tight supply now sits where traders usually buy or sell. Since that time long ago, movement has changed shape without loud announcements. Fewer coins available here means something, even if unspoken. Surging traffic across Base, Arbitrum, and Optimism isn’t just speeding things up – each transaction quietly eats into Blob Space. That steady drain is tightening supply, nudging ETH toward scarcity without fanfare. While chains hum louder, the ripple shows up where few expect: deeper down in Ethereum’s economic rhythm. Here’s what experts are watching closely now. A key threshold sits at $2,450 for Ethereum. Should the price stay above that mark past early May, momentum could build fast. The monthly chart shows a formation that traders recognize easily. That shape often leads to moves up, not sideways. Next stop might come in around 3,200. From there, room exists to stretch toward 4,100 before June ends. Opposite really. Selling might seem negative, yet Buterin trades erratic, nearly pointless memecoins into Ethereum instead. That move pulls real demand toward ETH, draining harmful liquidity out. Funding vital research happens along the way, strengthening the network deep down. Out of nowhere, big crypto wallets shift cash – straight from cold storage into staking deals. These moves suggest giants aren’t rushing out anymore. Instead, they’re holding tight, treating ETH like steady income tools built to last. One thing stands out: Bitmine holds 3.45 percent of the total ETH supply, shaping much of the network’s backbone. Because they back the long-term outlook so firmly, prices tend to stabilize even when markets shake hard. Smaller players benefit quietly, shielded from sudden crashes by that steady presence. Though control concentrated in one place raises eyebrows now and then, confidence grows because actions match words over time. Out past the current limits, Verkle Trees step in. These structures unlock what folks call stateless validation – meaning one day, Ethereum might handle countless transactions each second through its layered networks. Even then, anyone could still operate a personal node at home, no supercomputer needed. Most big moves on Lookonchain show clear actions – like Whale A sending $50 million to Binance – but leave the reasons open. Instead of jumping to conclusions, each person must decide what it means. By 2026, patterns point toward institutions adjusting holdings or locking tokens away. These shifts aren’t just panic selling; context matters more now. Title: Meta posted its best quarter ever. The stock dropped 9 per cent.
URL: https://thenextweb.com/news/meta-q1-2026-user-decline-ai-capex/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T18:58:42Z
Full Content:
TL;DRMeta reported record Q1 2026 revenue of $56.31 billion (+33%) and net income of $26.8 billion (+61%), but $8.03 billion of the profit came from a one-time tax benefit. Daily active people declined 5% sequentially to 3.56 billion, the first user decline in Meta’s history as a combined family of apps, attributed to the Iran war and a WhatsApp ban in Russia. The stock fell 9% after hours as Meta raised capex guidance to $125-145 billion while cutting 8,000 jobs. Meta reported record Q1 2026 revenue of $56.31 billion (+33%) and net income of $26.8 billion (+61%), but $8.03 billion of the profit came from a one-time tax benefit. Daily active people declined 5% sequentially to 3.56 billion, the first user decline in Meta’s history as a combined family of apps, attributed to the Iran war and a WhatsApp ban in Russia. The stock fell 9% after hours as Meta raised capex guidance to $125-145 billion while cutting 8,000 jobs. Meta posted the most profitable quarter in its history and the stock dropped 9 per cent. Revenue rose 33 per cent to $56.31 billion, beating Wall Street’s estimate of $55.49 billion. Net income reached $26.8 billion, up 61 per cent year over year. And for the first time since Meta began reporting its “family” of apps as a single unit, the number of people using Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger on a daily basis declined. Daily active people fell more than 5 per cent sequentially to 3.56 billion, missing the 3.62 billion that analysts expected. The company that built the largest social network in history is now growing its profits faster than its audience, spending more on artificial intelligence infrastructure than any other company on earth, and cutting 8,000 workers whose jobs it has decided machines will do better. The market looked at all of this and concluded that the best quarter Meta has ever reported is not good enough for what Meta is becoming. The headline figures are unambiguous. Revenue of $56.31 billion in the first quarter represents Meta’s strongest top-line performance ever, driven by a 19 per cent increase in ad impressions and a 12 per cent rise in the average price per ad. The advertising machine that funds the entire operation is running at full capacity. But the net income figure requires an asterisk. Of the $26.8 billion Meta reported, $8.03 billion came from a one-time tax benefit resulting from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, the Trump administration’s tax reform legislation that adjusted the treatment of previously capitalised research and development costs under the Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax. Without that benefit, net income was $18.7 billion and earnings per share were $7.31 rather than the reported $10.44. The quarter was still excellent by any historical standard. But a third of the profit improvement was a tax windfall, not an operational gain, and that distinction matters when investors are being asked to fund $145 billion in capital spending. The user decline is what spooked the market. Meta reported 3.56 billion daily active people across its family of apps in March 2026, up 4 per cent year over year but down more than 5 per cent from the fourth quarter of 2025. The company attributed the sequential drop to internet disruptions caused by the Iran war and a government-imposed restriction on WhatsApp access in Russia. Chief financial officer Susan Li said on the earnings call that absent these two factors, daily active people would have grown quarter over quarter. That may well be true. But it is also the first time Meta has had to explain away a decline in its user base, and the explanation itself is revealing: the company’s growth is now exposed to geopolitical events in markets where it has no ability to influence outcomes. A war and a government ban erased the equivalent of roughly 190 million daily users in a single quarter. Whether those users return depends on events in Tehran and Moscow, not Menlo Park. Meta raised its full-year 2026 capital expenditure guidance to between $125 billion and $145 billion, up from the $115 billion to $135 billion range it had guided just three months earlier. The increase, which the company attributed to higher component pricing and expanded data centre capacity, means Meta will spend more on infrastructure this year than the gross domestic product of more than 130 countries. The money is going into Nvidia GPUs, custom chips from Amazon and Broadcom, data centres that now require their own power plants, and the compute foundation for what Mark Zuckerberg has called “personal superintelligence.” Meta has committed to building several generations of custom MTIA processors with Broadcom on a 2-nanometre process, signed a $27 billion infrastructure deal with Nebius, and is exploring space-based solar energy to power facilities whose electricity demands exceed what terrestrial grids can reliably supply. TNW City Coworking space - Where your best work happens A workspace designed for growth, collaboration, and endless networking opportunities in the heart of tech. Reality Labs, the division responsible for the Quest headsets, Ray-Ban Meta glasses, and the company’s augmented and virtual reality ambitions, posted $402 million in revenue and a $4.03 billion operating loss. Cumulative Reality Labs losses since Meta began breaking out the unit now exceed $90 billion. The division has absorbed rolling layoffs throughout 2026, with its budget cut by 30 per cent in earlier rounds before the company announced the broader 8,000-person reduction that will take effect on 20 May. Zuckerberg has not abandoned the metaverse thesis, but the capital allocation tells the story more clearly than any earnings call rhetoric: AI infrastructure is getting $145 billion, and Reality Labs is getting budget cuts. The layoffs are the clearest signal of where Meta believes its value will come from. The company announced on 23 April that it would cut approximately 8,000 employees, roughly 10 per cent of its 78,865-person workforce, with additional reductions planned for the second half of 2026. The cuts span Reality Labs, the Facebook social division, recruiting, sales, and global operations. Combined with earlier rounds in January and March that eliminated around 2,200 positions, and the 21,000 employees cut in 2022 and 2023, Zuckerberg has now reduced Meta’s headcount by approximately 25,000 people since the post-pandemic contraction began. The pattern is consistent: every quarter, the company reports higher revenue, higher profits, and fewer employees. The savings are redirected into AI infrastructure that Meta believes will generate more value than the workers it replaced. The strategic logic is embodied in Meta Superintelligence Labs, the unit Zuckerberg created after spending $14.3 billion to acquire a 49 per cent stake in Scale AI and installing its founder, Alexandr Wang, as chief AI officer. The lab’s first model, Muse Spark, launched on 8 April as a closed-source, natively multimodal reasoning system that now powers Meta AI across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, and the Ray-Ban glasses. The decision to make Muse Spark closed-source, breaking from Meta’s Llama open-source tradition, reflects a calculation that the company’s most capable AI systems are too valuable to give away. Wang’s team, which includes five founders hired from Thinking Machines Lab at costs reportedly exceeding $1.5 billion for a single engineer, is building toward what Zuckerberg described as “delivering superintelligence to billions of people.” The ambition is not incremental. It is a bet that Meta’s future revenues will come not from showing more ads to more people, but from providing AI services so capable that they become essential infrastructure in their own right. The market’s reaction to Meta’s first quarter was not about the quarter itself. Revenue beat expectations. Advertising growth was strong. Even the user decline came with a plausible geopolitical explanation. What drove the stock down 9 per cent after hours was the forward-looking picture: a company that is increasing its spending commitments by $10 billion per quarter while its user base contracts, its metaverse division bleeds $4 billion every three months, and its most profitable line item was a one-time tax benefit that will not recur. Meta guided second-quarter revenue of $58 billion to $61 billion, which at the midpoint would represent 25 per cent growth. That is still exceptional for a company of Meta’s size. But the capex guidance of $125 billion to $145 billion means Meta expects to spend roughly $2.30 on infrastructure for every dollar of revenue it generates this year. The question investors are asking is not whether Meta can afford this. It clearly can, with $70.2 billion in cash and equivalents on its balance sheet. The question is whether the AI systems being built with that capital will produce returns that justify the expenditure before the advertising business, which funds everything, begins to slow. Zuckerberg’s answer is that the AI systems will become the advertising business. Meta AI, powered by Muse Spark, is already being integrated into product surfaces across the family of apps, and the company has said that AI-generated content recommendations now drive a significant share of engagement on Facebook and Instagram. The thesis is that better AI produces better content recommendations, which produce higher engagement, which produces more ad impressions at higher prices, which funds more AI. It is a closed loop, and the first-quarter results suggest it is working: ad impressions up 19 per cent, price per ad up 12 per cent, total ad revenue up 33 per cent. But the user decline introduces a variable that the loop does not account for. If the number of people seeing those ads is shrinking, even temporarily, the growth has to come entirely from showing more ads to fewer people at higher prices. That model has a ceiling, and Meta has not yet demonstrated that AI can raise it. The best quarter in the company’s history was also the first in which it had to explain why fewer people are using its products. For a company spending $145 billion on the premise that AI will make its products indispensable, that is a contradiction it will need to resolve with more than a tax benefit and a geopolitical footnote. Cristian Dina is the CRO at The Next Web. He has interviewed 300+ industry leaders and authored the book King of Networking, establishing hi (show all) Cristian Dina is the CRO at The Next Web. He has interviewed 300+ industry leaders and authored the book King of Networking, establishing himself as one of the most connected and respected voices in the ecosystem. At just 23 years old, Cristian was included in the Forbes 30 Under 30 2025 list, representing a new generation of tech builders, bold thinkers who move fast, build with purpose, and create real impact. Title: 'That entire story is false!' Irate Pete Hegseth shouts down Elizabeth Warren
URL: https://www.rawstory.com/hegseth-2676836269/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T18:55:33Z
Full Content:
Tom Boggioni is a writer, born, raised and living in San Diego — where he attended San Diego State University. Prior to writing for Raw Story, he wrote for FireDogLake, blogged as TBogg, and worked in banking, marketing and construction. Sen. Elizabeth Warren’s ability to grill Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth during her five minutes of questioning during a Senate Armed Forces Committee hearing was hampered by his raising his voice and loudly talking over her on Thursday afternoon. The Massachusetts Democrat got under Hegseth’s skin when she cited a Financial Times report that the Pentagon chief’s stockbroker attempted to make a “multimillion-dollar investment” in defense stocks before the invasion of Iran. After pressing him about growing concerns about insiders at the Pentagon possibly being involved in massive purchases on the stock market related to the war, which he dismissed out of hand, she brought up his personal finances. “I'm also concerned about recent reporting on your own financial dealings with regard to profiting from the war in Iran,” she began. “The Financial Times reported that your broker tried to buy hundreds of shares in a BlackRock fund invested in defense companies just before the war began. The law clearly prohibits the secretary —.” "That entire story is false!” Hegseth shouted. “[It] has been from the beginning and was made up out of whole cloth. And anybody that looks at it sees how it was worded from the beginning to make it look like I was involved in something I had nothing to do and never have.” As Warren tried to speak, he continued to talk over her, insisting, “So any insinuation that I've ever profited other than serving this nation, what I give, what you give, what others give. I'm not looking for money. I don't do it for money. I don't do it for profit. I don't do it for stocks. And that's part of the reason why I'm able to be effective in this job, because no one owns me.” “No one owns this department, no one owns this president. And we can execute for the American people. And we do,” he added. “So, I'd like to hear you say, did you, through your broker at Morgan Stanley or otherwise, seek to invest in any defense-related funds right before Trump started the Iran war?” she finally asked. “I'll give it to you as a big fat negative,” Hegseth shot back. - YouTube youtu.be ALSO READ: Ultra right-winger linked to $50M funneled through beloved charity to fund extreme agenda Copyright © 2026 Raw Story Media, Inc. Title: Bangkok's Most Anticipated Leadership Summit Just Got Bigger — Vanguard Impact Summit & Awards Adds May 24 Date After May 3 Sells Out
URL: https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/30/3285358/0/en/Bangkok-s-Most-Anticipated-Leadership-Summit-Just-Got-Bigger-Vanguard-Impact-Summit-Awards-Adds-May-24-Date-After-May-3-Sells-Out.html

April 30, 2026 14:28 ET | Source: Vanguard Impact Summit & Awards (VISA) Vanguard Impact Summit & Awards (VISA) Monarch Fusion Concepts confirms a second Bangkok edition of VISA 2026 on public demand — weeks after GISA 2026 Bali honoured 40 global leaders and Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine launched its first-ever quarterly print edition BANGKOK, April 30, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- What began as a single sold-out day has become a movement. Monarch Fusion Concepts, the event wing of Mavilach Group, has confirmed May 24, 2026, as a second Bangkok date for the Vanguard Impact Summit & Awards (VISA) 2026 — one of Asia's most credibility-driven global leadership summits and awards galas. The May 3, 2026 programme reached full capacity before the organising team could accommodate every leader who had been identified, invited, and confirmed. Rather than turn away qualified nominees, Monarch Fusion Concepts opened a second date — extending the platform to a wider cohort of global founders, CXOs, investors, and social impact leaders who earned their place through the same merit-based curatorial process. The announcement follows the April 15 release of the inaugural quarterly print edition of Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine — the permanent editorial record of the Global Impact Summit & Awards (GISA) 2026, held in Bali, Indonesia in February. The magazine is now in the hands of 1.5 million+ verified global decision-makers across 190+ countries. VISA 2026 is curated by Monarch Fusion Concepts, produced by Mavilach Group, in association with Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine and Unified Nations Council. "The response after Bali exceeded everything we had anticipated. The May 3 programme filled before we could accommodate everyone who had been identified and invited. Opening a second date on May 24 was not a commercial decision — it was the only responsible way to honour the number of leaders who deserved a seat in that room." — Ratnakar Mavilach, Group CEO, Mavilach Group | Asia Pacific Lead, Global Impact Awards. GISA 2026 Bali: Where It All Began — February 24–25, 2026 The Global Impact Summit & Awards (GISA) 2026 took place on February 24–25, 2026 at Harper Kuta Bali, Indonesia — the inaugural edition of a platform built on one principle: recognition must be earned, not purchased. Eighteen leaders were felicitated on stage. Twenty-two were honoured virtually. Four keynote speakers — Susan Xiong, Dr. Syed A. Kazmi, Leslie Venetz, and Dr. Sabira Arefin — addressed delegates on leadership, AI governance, ethical technology, and the future of B2B sales. Leaders recognised virtually — each featured in the April 2026 edition of Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine: Akash M Dubey (VP & AI Data Product Lead, JPMorgan Chase | USA) — shapes enterprise-scale AI and data platforms within Consumer and Community Banking, building decision systems grounded in governance-first design and institutional trust. Dr. Mansi S. Rai (Tax Auditor & Independent Researcher, New York State Department of Taxation and Finance | USA) — built a career at the intersection of public finance, independent scholarship, and civic conscience. Recipient of the Global Leadership Excellence Award in Public Sector Finance & Policy Innovation at GISA 2026. Simona Smaidžiūnaitė (Founder, Simona I AM | Melbourne, Australia) — built an international educational platform addressing women's health and bodily autonomy, filling gaps left by systems that routinely fail women at critical life junctures. May Goldi / Mee Goldsworthy (Founder, Velvet Reel Media | Director, Maeya TV | USA) — a media executive whose career spans Cantonese opera, Hollywood film set operations including The Last Samurai, luxury event production, and original digital programming. An operator's devotion to immaculate craft across every scale. Dr. Theresa L. Pringle-Cole (Founder, Pringle's Training Services | Newark, New Jersey, USA) — built a trauma-informed consultancy from the frontlines of housing instability, delivering workforce, life-skills, and housing-readiness training across multiple counties and internationally. Her principle: change the mindset, change the outcome. Mikhail Zubkov (Industrial Sustainability Engineer | Russia) — leads operational optimisation of gas processing systems handling 16.6 billion cubic metres annually without a single production shutdown, integrating energy efficiency, equipment reliability, and environmental performance into one unified framework. Stepan Arakelian (Technology Entrepreneur | Russia) — a software architect whose 15-year career spans algorithmic trading, industrial IoT, fintech, and gaming. Co-founded Treasury, a fintech platform combining e-commerce, anonymous auction infrastructure, and integrated payments — built to perform at scale under pressure. Petrova Ekaterina (Educator & Online Course Creator | Russia) — since 2022, built a structured digital education operation recognised for its systematic approach to knowledge transfer, scaling an individual teaching practice into a multi-course online platform. Georgii Shashiashvili (Director, NSO Industrial | Russia) — delivered a comprehensive design revision across thirteen regulatory sections for a 200,000 sq metre Wildberries warehouse complex in ten working days, on time, in full, with a ten-year warranty on every design solution. Daniil Pakalov (Organisational Transformation Leader | USA) — creator of the One DNA framework, a leadership and operations system tested across a 700-person telecommunications company in Florida that unifies leadership behaviour, process accountability, and workforce alignment into a single integrated system. Dylan Smith (Bestselling Author & Motivational Speaker | Boca Raton, Florida, USA) — diagnosed with ADHD and dyslexia, Smith rebuilt his identity from the ground up at sixteen and turned lived adversity into a bestselling advocacy career. Named in the 40 Under 40. Stanley Slaczka (President & CEO, The United Diamonds Versus The Eagles Country | USA) — U.S. Army Combat Armor veteran turned award-winning author. Multiple international book awards, including Gold First Place at the Global Book Awards. His work — including The Structure of Perseverance — is built on one message: discipline and systems thinking are the architecture of lasting impact. Mikhail Karmanov (Environmental Engineer & ESG Compliance Specialist | Chelyabinsk, Russia) — former Deputy Head at Rosprirodnadzor, Russia's federal Environmental Supervision Authority. Spent over a decade holding heavy industry accountable through atmospheric dispersion modelling, environmental auditing, and regulatory science. "Every leader on that stage was selected through editorial research and jury evaluation — not through a form submission. We built this platform around credibility, not scale. This inaugural edition sets the standard for everything that follows." — Sanjay Singh, Editor-in-Chief, Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine The Jury That Made It Count Every recognition at GISA 2026 was determined by an independent seven-member jury panel, featured in the April 2026 edition of Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine. Sanjay Singh (Editor-in-Chief, Entrepreneurs' Diaries) chaired the jury, bringing editorial discipline and long-form evaluation standards. Ratnakar Mavilach (Group CEO, Mavilach Group) brought a decade of cross-continental leadership platform expertise. Swapnil Devre (CMO, Mavilach Group) contributed a lens on public impact and narrative credibility. Angelina Shyltsyna (Financial Advisor, Barnum Financial Group | New York) — FINRA-licensed, internationally published researcher, and Innovator of the Year at a global conference spanning 20 countries, all before the age of twenty-five. Andrii Gerashchenko (Master Hairstylist & Educator, Luxbae Salon & Bokaos Aveda | Los Angeles) — seventeen years of international craft and a client list spanning Hollywood entertainers, musicians, and broadcast personalities. Mikhail Karmanov (Environmental Engineer, former Deputy Head at Rosprirodnadzor | Russia) — a decade at the intersection of heavy industry and ecological accountability. Emil Mirzaliyev (Senior Finance Executive | Azerbaijan & Scandinavia) — oversaw a €300 million FDI, the largest outside oil and gas in Azerbaijan's history, and engineered a debt repayment 24 months ahead of schedule. Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine — April 2026 Edition: Now in Global Circulation Released on April 15, 2026, the April edition is the platform's first-ever quarterly print issue and the permanent editorial record of GISA 2026. It carries full profiles of all recognised leaders, the debut of The Entrepreneurs' Diaries List — 30 global leaders selected through structured jury review — and business intelligence across venture capital, AI, global markets, and entrepreneurship in 2026. Karim Ali & Sarabjeet Kaur (Co-Founders, STEMOX | USA) grace the cover. Karim Ali spent nearly three decades delivering 250+ commercial real estate developments across the United States before founding STEMOX — a plant-based wellness brand built on the same discipline of execution that defined his real estate career. Sarabjeet Kaur joined as Co-Founder in November 2025, bringing global investment expertise and operational scaling architecture from her work across five international markets as CEO of Hamptons Investments INC. Leslie Venetz (Founder, The Sales-Led GTM Agency | Chicago, USA) — USA Today Top 50 bestselling author of Profit Generating Pipeline, 6x Global Top 50 B2B Sales Thought Leader, and keynote speaker whose content has reached 100 million+ views worldwide. She walked away from a $460,000 corporate offer to build a sales transformation agency that has opened doors to Walmart, JPMorgan, and KraftHeinz for her clients. At GISA 2026, she delivered a keynote that challenged global leaders to earn the right to every room they enter. Steph Adams (Author & Publisher | International) — With 30+ published titles and a nine-year passion project that landed an interview with Clint Eastwood, Adams turned the written word into one of the most quietly powerful empowerment platforms in international publishing. Her #1 international bestseller Powerhouse: A Legacy chronicles sixty-five global icons — from Hollywood legend Clint Eastwood and F1 Academy head Susie Wolff to supermodel Elle Macpherson and European royalty. VISA 2026 Bangkok — May 24: One Day. One Stage. No Shortcuts. The May 24 edition of VISA 2026 follows the same format and curatorial standard that defined every edition since Bali. The programme runs from 10:00 AM through an open-ended gala evening and includes three keynotes, four expert panels, live stage experiences, the Impact Ramp Walk fashion show, and the Vanguard Impact Awards Ceremony — honouring leaders across 50+ categories spanning business, technology, sustainability, social impact, and culture. Every delegate is personally identified and invited. Every award is granted on merit by an independent jury. Selected awardees receive an on-stage trophy and certificate, a dedicated feature in Entrepreneurs' Diaries Magazine, PR distribution across 200+ international publications, social media amplification across 190+ countries, permanent online indexing, and an official awardee digital badge and title. Title: Everyone’s Talking About NVIDIA. Smart Money Is Watching CrowdStrike Instead
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/04/30/everyones-talking-about-nvidia-smart-money-is-watching-crowdstrike-instead/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T18:27:31Z
Full Content:
Investing CrowdStrike (CRWD) reported Q4 ARR of $5.25B, up 24% YoY with record net new ARR of $330.7M (+47% YoY), while Falcon Flex ARR surged 120% YoY to $1.69B. NVIDIA (NVDA) trades at 44x trailing earnings with a $5.26T market cap and Polymarket traders pricing in a ceiling of $220 by April. Microsoft (MSFT) enables CrowdStrike Falcon purchases through Azure Marketplace, creating a distribution advantage. CrowdStrike is the security infrastructure required for every enterprise AI deployment, providing the same tailwind as NVIDIA but at a different valuation multiple and with significantly lower analyst coverage. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and CrowdStrike wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © insta_photos / Shutterstock.com NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA | NVDA Price Prediction) is up 96.1% over the past year and just crossed a $5.26 trillion market cap on the same agentic AI thesis Evercore and Wedbush keep recycling around Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)’s 50-day moving average. But here’s what you should actually be watching. The case against chasing NVIDIA at this level is straightforward. The stock trades at 44 times trailing earnings, every long-only fund on the planet already owns it, and management itself flagged that no China data center compute revenue is assumed in Q1 FY27 guidance. Polymarket traders are already pricing in the ceiling: a 0.321 probability of NVDA finishing April above $220 and only a 0.069 probability of $230. That distribution describes a consensus trade with a price cap stamped on it. Jensen Huang’s “agentic AI inflection point has arrived” line is true and already fully reflected in a stock that compounded 1,294% over five years. The redirect is CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD), sitting at down 2.94% year to date against NVIDIA’s 14.31% YTD gain. Three reasons that gap closes. One: CrowdStrike is the security tax on the AI buildout. CEO George Kurtz framed it directly on the most recent call: “As enterprises rapidly adopt AI, CrowdStrike is mission-critical infrastructure, securing AI across every layer from GPU to agent to prompt.” Every dollar that flows into NVIDIA hardware has to be defended in production. Q4 FY26 ending ARR reached $5.25 billion, up 24% YoY, with record net new ARR of $330.7 million, up 47% YoY. The growth is accelerating into FY27. Two: the Microsoft tie-in the title actually points to. CrowdStrike’s expanded strategic alliance with Microsoft enables Falcon purchases via Azure Marketplace, and AWS named it the first cybersecurity partner via SaaS Quick Launch in AWS Marketplace. When analysts cheer Microsoft’s chart, they are cheering a distribution channel CrowdStrike plugs directly into. Three: Falcon literally runs on the NVIDIA stack. Charlotte AI AgentWorks was built with NVIDIA technologies, and Falcon Cloud Security is integrated with NVIDIA NIM microservices and NeMo Safety. Same tailwind, different layer, different multiple. The fundamentals back the redirect. Q4 free cash flow came in at $376.36 million, a 29% margin. Falcon Flex ARR reached $1.69 billion, up over 120% YoY. Platform consolidation is visible across the customer base: 50% of customers run six or more modules, 34% run seven, 24% run eight. Management guided FY27 revenue to $5.87 billion to $5.93 billion, with a long-term aspiration of $20 billion in ending ARR by FY36. Keybanc moved to Overweight on April 21 with a $525 price target, and the analyst consensus target sits at $491.72 against a current price of $454.99. Reddit activity on CrowdStrike over the past month registered as “very_low” to “low” with only one to two qualified mentions per observation period. That silence is the tell. A retirement portfolio gets paid for owning the quiet, profitable company structurally tied to the loud trend everyone else is overpaying to access through one mega-cap ticker. Move CrowdStrike to the top of your research list before the consensus catches up. Our top personal finance-related articles today. Your wallet will thank you later. NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA | NVDA Price Prediction) is up 96.1% over the past year and just crossed a $5.26 trillion… CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD | CRWD Price Prediction) currently trades at $426.51, while the Wall Street consensus price target sits at… Jim Cramer used his Mad Money segment on April 21 to argue that investors who dumped CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD |… Anthropic’s Claude Mythos Preview demonstrated that AI can now surpass all but the most skilled humans at finding and exploiting… Benchmark analyst Yi Fu Lee initiated coverage of CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD | CRWD Price Prediction) with a Buy rating and… Wall Street is sending a split but net-bullish signal on CRWD after CrowdStrike’s record fourth quarter. Two firms raised price… An analyst firm just put its stamp of approval on cybersecurity leader CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD | CRWD Price Prediction). Mizuho upgraded… CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD | CRWD Price Prediction) stock is sliding hard this morning. CRWD shares are down 7% on Friday, trading around… CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD | CRWD Price Prediction) stock is trading up 4% in Monday afternoon trading, with shares around $383 after opening…

Title: The Outlaws Who Keep the Power Grid Running
URL: https://www.esquire.com/news-politics/a71078454/power-grid-outlaws/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T16:33:14Z
Full Content:
Over the last decade, an army of hard-living adrenaline junkies made a fortune and found a kind of freedom maintaining the nation’s energy infrastructure. It was good while it lasted. Last August, in Seminole, Florida, a suburb of St. Petersburg, a crew of power linemen were replacing a utility pole at a small electrical substation next to a used-car dealership. The temperature was pushing 90 degrees, but the sky was clear; an ideal Florida beach day. Just before 2:00 p.m., they strapped the replacement metal pole to the boom on their truck and hoisted it vertically into the air. Setting a new pole is an act of both muscularity and balletic precision, but for experienced linemen, it’s a typical task. The new pole must be held steady at the bottom while an operator in the truck uses a claw on the end of the boom to maneuver it into a waiting hole. When done right, linemen can replace multiple utility poles in a day, but things in Seminole didn’t go as planned: When the boom operator attempted to grab the pole with the claw, he bumped against it instead, causing the top of the pole to accidentally make contact with the high-voltage transmission lines running overhead. The explosion that followed was so violent it lit the earth itself on fire. Sixty-nine thousand volts, enough to feed the substation, powering entire neighborhoods, diverted from the wire into the new pole and rocketed toward the ground. In the current’s way stood Justin Bryan, a thirty-year-old father and music director at his local church, who was balancing the pole from below. The current ripped through Bryan’s body, killing him. It continued flowing into the ground around the truck, causing the grassy field to burst into flames, smoke billowing skyward. Two other linemen, Jefferey Busch and Matthew Waldrop, fathers in their late twenties and early thirties, were also in contact with the pole and were left crawling on the charred earth, horrifically burned but alive. Bystanders could do little to help; so long as the wires remained energized, electricity could still arc and flow anywhere in the vicinity. I first saw an eyewitness video of the accident’s horrific aftermath this past December, not long after I had arrived at the headquarters of Pack Power Services, a utility-services company nestled among the horse farms of Ohio Amish country. Work had been slow; a thin dusting of snow coated the few dozen bucket trucks and pickups in the lot outside the company’s warehouse. When I started reporting on the lives of linemen late last year, I quickly discovered that although linemen are on the front lines of maintaining the power grid—our connection to medical care, drinking water, cell phones, the Internet, emergency services, lights, heat, you name it—no one knows much about them. Most people assumed I was writing about football. But the people who did know what a lineman was kept telling me to talk to the same person: Pack Power’s owner, Jerry Pack. Colleagues told me that he was the quintessential lineman—an Army vet and a biker; rough around the edges, sure, but loyal to a fault. But he was also more than an archetype. Jerry had helped revolutionize the trade and ridden its ups and downs for years. When I got in touch, Jerry, who looks a bit like the actor Brendan Gleeson if Brendan Gleeson had a graying ZZ Top beard and tattoos down his forearms, invited me to come visit on a Thursday—“That’s when we drink,” he said—and greeted me at the warehouse door with a lit Marlboro Red between his fingers. He had also invited a few other linemen with stories to tell, including Nick Mansfield, from East Texas, who quickly pulled me aside and asked if I wanted to see the “real shit” about being a lineman. He pulled his cell phone from his pocket and hit play on a copy of the Seminole video. I lasted only a few seconds before I had to look away. Nick, who met Jerry years ago on the job and remains one of his closest friends, is a lineman’s lineman. He’s burly and bearded and wears a chain around his neck that, at first glance, appears to be a traditional Christ on the cross but isn’t Jesus at all. Rather, it’s a lineman climbing a utility pole. While the incident in Seminole was barely more than a blip in the local news, he told me linemen across the country had seen the video. There’s an old expression, he said: “Telegraph, telephone, tell a lineman.” Word travels fast in the industry, and the industry, lately, had heard too many stories like this. A lineman dies on the job on average once every two weeks, ranking it among the deadliest professions in America. While Jerry and Nick showed me around the warehouse, we discussed the incident in Seminole. The Pinellas County Sheriff’s Office investigated and determined it was an accident, while OSHA recently levied $49,650 in penalties, which is being contested by the contractor hired by the utility company, Duke Energy. But as the video made its way from the jobsite to the union hall to TikTok, linemen in the comments section became fixated on the fact that the transmission line was energized during the work. Jerry and Nick told me that they would have demanded the utility de-energize the lines before getting started, a precaution that would have stopped all the meters in all the homes and businesses downstream of the substation from spinning—and from generating revenue. (When contacted about the details of the incident, Duke Energy replied with this statement: “The safety of our employees, contractors and customers remains Duke Energy’s highest priority, and we continue to cooperate fully with the ongoing investigation process.”) Jerry understood, all too well, why so many linemen who saw the video scrutinized the safety practices that were in place. The men in Seminole had been doing what’s called “blue sky” work, regular repair and maintenance of the power grid. But the industry’s relentless push toward high speed and low cost were most evident in the niche kind of linework that Jerry specialized in: storm chasing. In this run-and-gun model of linework, independent crews show up whenever and wherever they are needed to get the power back on after hurricanes, blizzards, and wildfires. Multiple linemen described the storm-chasing world to me as the “Wild West,” a place where the allure of big money attracted roguish personalities. For years, the living was good, great even, but to hear Jerry and Nick tell it, the frontier was closing. America’s electrical grid was becoming more critical to daily life than ever, and the opportunity to rake in profits had attracted middleman contractors and private-equity investment. For the storm chasers, that meant pressure to perform ratcheting ever higher, and their livelihoods coming under threat as the incentives to do whatever it took to secure contracts—aboveboard or otherwise—grew stronger. For the rest of us? It meant the future of the grid itself was at stake. As the sun went down, the three of us drove the few minutes to Jerry’s ranch house, where we settled in around the kitchen table. Jerry’s girlfriend, Rita, who had already set out foil trays filled with pigs in a blanket and chicken wings on the stovetop, offered us pint glasses of vodka and cranberry juice. I asked Jerry when things started to change. “About ten years ago,” he said as he filled a paper plate with wings. “Irma was the fire under my ass.” In 2017, Jerry was pulling into his driveway after work one day when he discovered his then-wife, Sally, waiting in the doorway in tears. “It’s cancer,” she told him. Her doctor had just called with the test results they’d been waiting for. Over the previous eighteen years, Jerry had worked his way up from an apprenticeship to doing blue-sky work at a substation as a contractor for a local utility. It was a good job with a regular schedule. Sally’s diagnosis changed everything. He’d need to be available to drive her to treatment and support her when she got home. He decided on the spot to drag up—quit without notice, in lineman-speak—and take to the road. “I’ll just storm-chase,” he told her. At the time, most power-restoration efforts were handled by mutual-aid agreements between utilities. If a hurricane hit Florida, a utility company in North Carolina might send some crews to help, understanding that Florida would return the favor if needed. A few large private energy companies had their own crews. Jerry started putting in calls. If linework is the Wild West, storm chasers are the outlaws of the bunch. They’re the guys lauded as heroes on local-news broadcasts for restoring power after natural disasters, but among their ranks you can find all kinds, from drug-addled ex-felons to former lawyers and everything in between. Some storm chasers, affectionately known as tramps, essentially live on the road full-time, traveling the country in RVs or tricked-out pickup trucks, going wherever they’re needed. They keep two drag bags packed at all times: one for their gear—harnesses, hard hats, and boot spikes for climbing utility poles—and the other stuffed with a week’s worth of clothes. Walker Pettis, a tramp from Washington, told me he’s moved thirty times in the past five years. No sooner had Jerry decided to join their ranks than the strongest hurricane in recent memory began intensifying in the southern Atlantic. The meteorologists called it Irma. “THIS IS AS REAL AS IT GETS,” wrote the National Weather Service in a tweet the night before Irma struck. Residents were urged to flee. In the largest exodus in American history, some seven million Floridians got in their cars and drove north, leaving their homes, businesses, and belongings behind. Jerry, on the other hand, jumped in a truck and headed south toward Miami, amid a caravan of vehicles carrying linemen from across the country. He had gotten a call from an acquaintance asking if he could lead a crew. At his day job before storms, Jerry usually ran with four other guys; in Florida, he’d be in charge of 120. “I was scared to fucking death, dude,” he said. In Miami, Jerry and his crew holed up in a hotel and watched through the windows as Irma transformed the world around them: waves of water breaching the Miami River seawall, palm trees blown horizontal in the gale, alligators wandering suburban sidewalks. Nearly two thirds of the state was left without power. Some sixty thousand linemen had descended on the state, and the utility companies scrambled to get organized. The linemen were happy to wait; they were getting paid whether they left the hotel or not. Chaos is cash, as linemen like to say, and Irma had brought the chaos. President Trump described the response as “the largest assemblage of human beings ever in one area for power.” Linemen faced floodwaters, live wires dangling amid the trees, and bird dogs from the utility companies—the office workers tasked with directing crews to their next work site—pressuring them to work on high-voltage lines in gusts of wind and rain. While they moved from one downed pole or blown-out transformer to the next, Jerry and his crew were approached by elderly couples desperate for help and by countless residents demanding to have their TVs turned back on. In a little over a week, the linemen had fully restored power in a state of twenty million people. Public officials hailed them as heroes. Jerry didn’t feel like a hero until he deposited his paycheck. His stress finally abated. “Don’t sweat it,” he told Sally. “We’re all set.” Irma changed everything for Jerry, and it changed everything for linemen. The traditional mutual-aid model was effective, but it could be slow. Pack Power, which Jerry started after returning from Irma, was one of the country’s first linemen-owned businesses to offer something different: rapid response. Jerry’s guys could be sent anywhere they were needed at a moment’s notice. Within a few years, a post-Irma surge of these kinds of outfits had revolutionized the industry. “Years ago, there were no companies that just did storm work,” said a stockbroker-turned-lineman who left what he described as the “scumbag business” of Wall Street and now runs a storm-recovery business on the East Coast. “Today, I’d only be guessing, but I would say there’s probably close to between six hundred to a thousand companies that do just that.” After we’d been talking for an hour, Randy Wolfe slipped into the kitchen. A slight, quiet guy in his early forties who lived nearby, he sat down on a kitchen stool and pulled a Modelo from some hidden recess of his lime-yellow reflective work jacket. Randy epitomized the stormchasing lineman of the post-Irma era, who were in control of their schedules and making fortunes unthinkable to their blue-sky brethren. His taste in beer may be lowbrow, but he owns a collection of high-end sports cars, including a C5 Corvette and a Lotus Elise. More than once, the quiet roads around Jerry’s house had been lit up by wailing sirens and flashing lights as Randy outran the cops in one of his supercars. Randy described the gaps between jobs like this: “I pick a car and I go to the lake all day. If the phone rings, fine. If it don’t ring, fantastic.” For this new generation of linemen, storm-chasing became as much a lifestyle as a livelihood, built on the twin pillars of money and freedom. “We’re like modern-day pirates,” said Nick. At first, all Jerry had wanted was the freedom to bring Sally to her oncology appointments in Chicago without worrying about sick days or PTO. But once the money started rolling in, he bought a few Porsches and some rental properties and expanded his fleet of bucket trucks. The indoor hot tub he installed at his house was so big he had to build a new room to go around it. In order to maintain this newfound abundance, storm chasers relied on a steady stream of what’s known in the business as Good Storms. What exactly makes a Good Storm is up for debate, though time is the obvious answer. A Good Storm lasts a couple weeks at least. The longest can go on for a month or more. For linemen working sixteen-hour days, seven days a week, a Good Storm can yield a paycheck on par with the average American annual salary. Jerry told me that during Irma—one of the best storms in recent memory—the most experienced guys on his crew, which was unionized, were being paid around $150 an hour, double or even triple the going rate back home. That’s $16,800 a week, not including food, travel, and a place to sleep. Though most linemen will proudly recount stories of residents thanking them for getting their power back on after a disaster, it’s clear they’re not in it for the goodwill alone. “This isn’t a humanitarian effort,” as one lineman told me. Nick’s been doing it for so long he now refers to the Weather Channel as “the Money Channel.” Linemen have been known to quit salaried positions without notice just to join a single storm roster during hurricane season. This wayward streak has long been associated with linework. Nick’s “line dad”—his mentor—told him that the roots of the linemen’s renegade spirit can be found in the Vietnam vets who returned from the war and joined the ranks because they felt out of place in society. But the storm-chasing lifestyle brought it to new heights. After driving long hours in caravans of bucket trucks, linemen ride out storms everywhere from empty hotel rooms to hammocks in the backs of their trucks to military-style tent-and-cot setups known as “man camps.” These overnights offer the chance for a lineman’s worst instincts to flourish—hard drinking, affairs with local women dubbed “bucket bunnies,” even crime. Linemen on storm have been accused of everything from felony assault to trafficking drug shipments in their trucks. “There is a culture in linework that is seedy, romanticized, drug ridden,” the former stockbroker said. “There’s a lot of guys that are looking for the next high.” Nick once had a crewmate who found himself face-to-face with an angry pimp after unceremoniously kicking a sex worker out of his hotel room in the middle of the night. A fight ensued and the pimp ended up both beaten up and under arrest. “The lineman was at work at seven in the fucking morning,” Nick recalled. And yet, despite all the potential for misadventure, the shared unpleasantness of sleepless nights and long waits for food at the mess tent had a way of turning colleagues into comrades. “We meet guys one time,” said Nick, “work a storm for a week, and you become lifelong friends.” Nick’s been sober for half a year now, and all night Jerry subtly topped him off with water, not wanting him to stare at an empty glass while we downed our cocktails. Linework was simply too dangerous to do anything but have each other’s back, Jerry told me. “Your ego can’t get in that.” Randy lit up describing how a crowd of linemen had gathered in awe and respect when he and his father ran into each other on storm and set a utility pole together. “It was one of the coolest things I’ve ever been a part of,” he said, pulling another Modelo from his vest. He’d keep drinking them all night. They were a merry band in those days. They saw themselves as something like outlaws, because they’d found a way to make money hand over fist living an itinerant, rambunctious lifestyle that suited them. They were getting away with something. But the hard-partying, carefree life was about to get a lot harder. “Those days are about gone,” Jerry said, wistfully. Someone with an even better way to turn chaos into cash was riding into town. Jaradda Sparks sensed an opportunity. The forward-thinking lineman from Alabama saw more and more storm chasers following Jerry in opening their own companies. He also saw utilities struggling to manage them. He went straight to the utility companies and promised to save them time and money by aggregating the services of hundreds of smaller storm-chasing companies under the umbrella of his own, Sparks Energy. If a storm hit, a power company only had to make one call, to Sparks, to secure the services of thousands of linemen spread out across the country. No more shopping or logistics; Sparks was a one-stop shop. He charged the utilities a hefty premium, but they were clearly willing to pay: By 2019, he had made enough money to open his own livestock ranch, specializing in breeding “top-caliber bucking bulls” for the rodeo circuit. This new aggregator model initially had a beneficial trickle-down effect. Sparks declined to speak with me for this article, but I heard from many linemen that while his approach was designed to cater to utilities, it also funneled jobs to storm chasers in his network, who could work dozens of storms a year without the legwork of going out and bidding for new business. Jerry grossed $15 million one of the years he worked under Sparks as a subcontractor. But the model was so successful that aggregators soon multiplied, just as the storm-chasing outfits once had, creating competition that made life harder for guys like Jerry. In order to lock in higher premiums from utility companies, aggregators tried to corner the market, securing ironclad contracts that ensured utilities would work only with them. Independent companies found it increasingly challenging to land contracts of their own. In intervening years, the industry took on a new shape. There was still a ton of money to be made, but now there was a lot more competition, a new layer of middlemen, and more complicated contracts. This environment cut into the bottom lines of companies like Pack Power. It also opened the door to bad actors. None of the dozen active linemen I spoke to for this story were willing to go on the record about corruption in the industry, but they’ve worked all over the country and nearly all had stories. One storm chaser told me a company he’d worked for hauled broken-down bucket trucks on wreckers to storms because they were being paid per truck, even if the trucks sat in a parking lot unused. Another lineman told me he had been sent out on a storm crew without the proper tools or dielectric testing to make sure the trucks were insulated in case of an accident. When the crews came back an hour later, unable to work, he still got paid. Less visible backroom deals were also in play. One storm-company owner told me explicitly that he paid $15,000 for an executive at a power company to go on an overseas trip in exchange for better work contracts, only for the executive to ghost him once he had returned. The cutthroat competition in the industry incentivized these tactics, and the problems were perpetuated when word, inevitably, got out. Telegraph, telephone, tell a lineman. These shifting practices may also have put lives at risk. At least one lineman I spoke to who asked not to be named told me that some aggregators were known for hiring “guys who weren’t qualified to do the work and going out with trucks that didn’t have any tooling on them.” Jerry’s business suddenly began to suffer. After a couple of years in the black, he found that he was getting fewer calls from aggregators. He told me that inexperienced, nonunion linemen were willing to work for less money per hour than his guys, brothers in the IBEW. Then it stopped being standard for aggregators to pay linemen for mobilization and travel days, forcing those costs to come out of Jerry’s bottom line instead. All this, and many questioned whether the aggregator model was actually saving anyone any money. Average electricity prices are up more than 30 percent across the country in the last five years alone. Are the changes in linework the reason why? It’s hard to say for certain—but it certainly hasn’t helped. Looking back, the storm chasers should have known the end of their era was near when the bucket bunnies broke the Internet. In 2022, a Florida TikToker revealed that in the weeks following Hurricane Ian, her Tinder account had become overrun with linemen heading to the Sunshine State for storm work. “Everyone saying pray for Florida but this is our tinder right now,” she wrote before the reel revealed that, extensive storm damage aside, things in the state maybe weren’t so bad: There was a stream of handsome, well-paid linemen in town looking for dates. Other users followed suit, posting videos of linemen mobilizing for work outside hotels and in parking lots. Then the “linewives,” the married partners of linemen, arrived, posting their own TikToks railing against the bucket bunnies who were out to steal their men. The melodrama was hugely popular. The bucket-bunny trend lasted for months and was viewed nearly three billion times, landing it just behind the Ice Bucket Challenge as one of the most popular memes of the past few years. It spawned merchandise; online commenters begged for a reality-TV show. The linemen, however, were unenthused. As word got out about how much money they were making, sleeping with women while on storm became a lot less carefree. Nick, in his inimitable way, took the opportunity to explain the other three T’s of linework: “You buy your girl new teeth, new tits, and a brand-new Tahoe, before she walks out on you,” he said. Some apprentices, he told me, stopped letting women know they were linemen. Social media had transformed bucket bunnies, at one time the consummate example of the linemen’s freewheeling lifestyle, into a new kind of peril, one that could lead to divorce or something even worse: child support. Of course, if enough money was flying around that bucket bunnies could sniff it out, it was only a matter of time before the real bloodhounds showed up: Wall Street. In the past few years alone, high-powered private-equity firms like BlackRock and Blackstone have found ways to get their cut. Attracted by the potential for a huge, data-center-driven increase in energy, they’ve spent billions buying up major utility providers in places like Minnesota, Indiana, New Mexico, and abroad. According to one count, some 150 million customers in the U. S. now rely on utilities funded at least in part by private-equity money. Under the PE model, buying into the companies that service those utilities is simply another layer of vertical integration to leverage. Just last year, Apollo Global Management spent roughly $2 billion to acquire a stake in PowerGrid Services, a prominent grid-repair and maintenance company founded by a former lineman in 2016. Investments like these are touted as necessary to improve the grid’s infrastructure. Last July, BlackRock’s CEO, Larry Fink, said as much in an interview on CNBC. “We believe that there’s a need for trillions of dollars investing in infrastructure related to our power grids, AI, the whole digitization of the economy,” said Fink. But how big money’s incursion into the grid will affect both linemen and ratepayers is less clear. According to industry studies, the average length of power outages in 2025 was 40 percent longer than in the three years prior. Part of that is down to the increasing severity and frequency of the storms themselves. But an experienced workforce of linemen is essential to the recovery effort. And for them, private equity has been even worse than for customers. In the last two years alone, nonunion linemen have seen pay shrink, while call times to mobilize for storms have shortened from a few days to a few hours. “In the beginning of my storm-chasing career,” Jerry explained, “you had a reasonable time to get there. See the lady, get packed up, take her out to eat. Get on a plane the next day.” Now, he continued, “I have to have guys at the shop ready to leave at all times.” This sometimes means defying DOT rules limiting the number of hours bucket-truck drivers can spend on the road. Once they’ve arrived, the already fatigued linemen are still expected to work sixteen-hour days, seven days a week, until the job is done. So far, linemen have been spared their Perfect Storm moment; there’s no mass tragedy that defines the trade. Instead, the specter of death arrives in a slow, steady cadence. In April of last year, a lineman was killed in Tennessee when his truck overturned into floodwaters; a few weeks later, four linemen were killed in North Carolina when a city dump truck crashed into their work site; in May, a falling tree in Alabama killed a lineman where he stood; in July, a lineman was electrocuted a few miles from where the Seminole tragedy unfolded a month later; in October, a hit-and-run; November, another electrocution, this time in Louisville. A number of fatal incidents show how pressure to cut corners and use junior crews can make the job even more perilous. In recent years, OSHA has assessed penalties of more than $40,000 and claimed that the lack of proper safety precautions led to at least two separate fatal incidents. In one, an electric company “failed to meet their responsibility to ensure a safe and healthful workplace and ensure the proper supervision of new workers.” That incident, in Alabama, resulted in the death of two nineteen-year-old apprentices. In the other, in Miami, a contractor “failed to use required safeguards before starting work,” and a thirty-two-year-old lineman died. Earlier in the evening, I had asked Jerry about the worst accident he had ever seen. He sighed and slumped slightly in his chair; Nick shot me a glance. Don’t go there. But when I brought it up again later, I got a different response: indignation at how the blame for injuries and deaths always seemed to fall at the feet of the linemen themselves, as opposed to, say, contractors or utility companies pressuring crews to work despite safety concerns. “Doesn’t matter what happens, who dies,” Nick said. “The problem can never get back to the power company.” Despite the contested fine levied by OSHA in the Seminole incident, the families of all three victims have had to fend for themselves, starting GoFundMe campaigns to help cover the cost of their recovery. It’s something Anne-Marie Olivieri, a linewife, has seen all too often. She founded the LineLife Foundation to help raise money for linemen killed on the job and as part of her effort tracks every injury and death she can, compiling lists and sharing them among linemen and their families. According to her, the tallies are rarely complete. “A lot of these are swept under the rug or they don’t get reported,” she told me. “There’s been times where I’ve actually gotten pictures of the accident and the company has threatened me.” Wary of a lawsuit, she declined to discuss the particulars, but her experience informs a fear among linemen that in this new private-equity world, shareholder profit, not outage times or lineman safety, will be the new benchmark for success. Nick, indelibly, described how linemen feel. After an accident, he said, “they’ll post your job before they post your obituary.” Around 11:00 p.m., Jerry pushed back from the kitchen table, stood up, and announced it was time to go to the hot-tub room. In there, Nick lit up a cigar while Jerry showed me his collection of Donald Trump–styled rubber duckies and bottles of Pappy Van Winkle bourbon. Like the Vietnam vets who had found a home in linework before him, Jerry had spun a decent day job into his own American dream: freedom, money, and the open road. But it was fleeting. The salad days of $15 million in gross are long gone. This article appeared in the April/May 2026 issue of Esquiresubscribe So far, Jerry is uninterested in cashing in on the private-equity money-go-round, partly, it seems, due to pride. Money and freedom, both valuable things, aren’t quite the same. “There was a time when I had to roll my own fucking cigarettes,” he told me. “I built this, I made every fucking bit of this.” He’d rather hand off Pack Power Services to his kids than to a Wall Street firm. Chris, his eldest son, is already in a leadership role, handling much of the company’s day-to-day responsibilities, including storm work. PJ, another son, is the in-house mechanic. Jerry’s daughter stands to inherit his rental properties. And while he and Sally, recovered from cancer, split up a few years ago, she lives just a few minutes away in a house Jerry says he paid for. This future is dependent on whether or not the company survives. Like nearly all the other businesses started by former linemen whose owners I spoke to, the injection of big money into the industry has made it difficult for Pack Power to compete. In the good ol’ days, Jerry had millions in the bank and no debt. “Now I’ve got fifty grand in the bank and I’m two million in the hole,” he told me. Jerry still has the lineman’s hustle in his blood. He’s working on a deal to help the FAA modernize the infrastructure at a nearby airport. And there’s this other thing. “As soon as I saw Russia invading Ukraine, I knew we, as the United States, were going to be rebuilding their power grid,” he told me. “I’d love to be on that.” I didn’t know how seriously to take him, considering we were surrounded by empty beer cans and a haze of cigar and cigarette smoke. But as it turns out, Jerry had in fact spent much of the past year developing plans with a partner in the UK to join the international effort to rebuild Ukraine’s power grid, whenever that day comes. It was nearing midnight when I left Jerry’s, the image of a crew of American linemen stepping off a plane in postwar Kyiv, just as they’d rushed into California after a wildfire or Florida after Irma, running through my head. The Wild West had been tamed, so they’d have to go find a new frontier. Outside, it was dark; there weren’t many streetlights in the neighborhood. But as I drove past the Pack Power warehouse on the way to my hotel, it was a different sight that stirred something in me: a long row of bucket trucks parked in the lot out front, ready and waiting for the next job, still coated in snow but illuminated under the warehouse lights. Title: Remember Twitter's CEO fired by Elon Musk? Parag Agrawal just built a $2 billion empire from scratch
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/international/us/remember-twitters-ceo-fired-by-elon-musk-parag-agrawal-just-built-a-2-billion-empire-from-scratch/articleshow/130639329.cms
